text: The surgeon places robotic ports a few centimeters below the umbilicus on the right, middle, and left, preparing for the robotic docking procedure.

The surgeon reduces the hernia contents, revealing necrotic omental tissue that breaks during manipulation. A flap is created above the umbilicus for a TAPP repair, aiming to cover a two to two and a half centimeter epigastric incisional defect. As the dissection progresses, unexpected findings alter the surgical plan.

The surgeon performs preperitoneal dissection above the midline fat, aiming to reach the anterior abdominal wall and posterior sheath. The hernia defect is visible, and the surgeon widens the flap to prevent instrument tearing during dissection. The bedside assistant applies pressure to the hernia defect, but necrotic and fibrotic tissue prevents further protrusion.

The surgeon dissects the left and right sides of the hernia defect, aiming to achieve the 'volcano sign' near the subxiphoid area. The hernia sac is heavily scarred due to chronic incarceration and fat necrosis, leading to its amputation. This results in a small defect, approximately the size of the hernia neck, which is easily closed later.

The surgeon observes omentum and necrotic fat descending with the hernia sac. The xiphoid bone is visible, revealing a defect from a previous sternotomy. The surgeon performs a subxiphoid dissection to create space for mesh placement, extending the flap superiorly to cover the defect and prevent potential hernia development.

The surgeon identifies small defects on the left and right sides of the xiphoid, resembling Morgagni hernias, during the subxiphoid dissection. The fat is reduced from the right defect, and the dissection is carried across the midline. The surgeon ensures the mesh will cover these areas, staying on the peritoneum without involving muscle. Due to the patient's anticoagulation, meticulous hemostasis is maintained. The surgeon decides to create more space superiorly under the diaphragm for a larger mesh than initially planned.

The surgeon performs central cephalad dissection and measures the pocket for mesh placement. A 25x25 Versatex mesh is cut to 20x17 and positioned beneath the subxiphoid sternal defect. The surgeon closes the defect, ensuring the mesh sits against tissue to prevent eventration. The needle is felt scraping along the periosteum and connective tissue without penetrating the bone. The epigastric incisional defect is closed transversely, with decreased insufflation pressure aiding the closure.

The surgeon runs a needle for additional security, placing a 20 by 17 Versatex mesh with a trio silk suture at the cranial aspect to secure it under the sternum against the diaphragm. Cardinal sutures are used to stabilize the mesh. The surgeon closes the wide mouth flap starting from the middle and moving to the patient's right, ensuring meticulous hemostasis due to the patient's anticoagulation. The left side of the flap is then closed using a 9-inch, 3-0 V-Loc suture, which also closes the defect where the hernia sac was amputated and other small peritoneal rents with Vicryl sutures.
